content.full_text:
February 3, 1990  
581 Rim Rock Road  
El Cajon, CA 92020

California Fair Political Practices Commission  
P.O. Box 807  
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Gentlemen:

I am a member of the City Council of the City of El Cajon. Several weeks ago a group of citizens appeared before the Council asking that the Council request the San Diego County Board of Supervisors to delete from the County's general plan the extension of a street that crosses the City and terminates presently at the City limits. These citizens, for the most part, are residents in the unincorporated area.

The request was precipitated by the El Cajon Cemetery Association which is located outside the City and which is interested in acquiring the land the street extension traverses in order to expand the cemetery. The extension of this street (Greenfield Drive) is in the unincorporated area but within the City of El Cajon's sphere of influence. I am enclosing a recent newspaper article about this.

When it became clear that the Cemetery Association was behind the request and would be impacted by the street extension, I abstained, since my husband and I own two plots in the cemetery.

Another Council member also abstained because his home is in the area. The remaining Council members could not agree on a course of action.

An attorney associated with the group of citizens questioned my abstaining. It is my practice to abstain if there is even an appearance of conflict of interest, and this is the first time anyone has ever questioned my decision to abstain.

To complicate the matter further, the street extension also appears on El Cajon's general plan in the sphere of influence area. As I understand the situation, the County has no current plans to construct the extension. However, if the residents are interested in getting it deleted entirely, it would need to be removed from the general plans of both the County of San Diego and the City of El Cajon.

So, it is possible that public hearings might be held later by both the El Cajon Planning Commission and the City Council about such a general plan amendment, and I would like to know if my abstention was proper and required or if I could participate in hearings that might occur later.

We paid $700.00 for these two plots in 1985. Lots in the cemetery are now selling for $150 each, so our two are probably currently worth $900.00.

Thank you for your assistance.

Harriet M. Stockwell  
Harriet M. Stockwell, Council Member  
City of El Cajon

Enclosure
